title: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
code: public static int SearchRotated(int[] nums, int target)
if (nums[lo] <= target && target < nums[mid]) hi = mid - 1;
if (nums[mid] < target && target <= nums[hi]) lo = mid + 1;

frames.caption: Initialize the closed interval: lo = 0, hi = 6. The array is sorted but rotated at a pivot between index 3 and 4.
frames.note: A rotated sorted array is not globally ordered, but here is the key structural fact: for any window [lo, hi], at least one half is still a clean sorted run. The pivot (the wrap point) can live in only one half, so the other half is untouched, contiguous order. That residual order is exactly what binary search needs — we just have to figure out which half is the sorted one before we decide where to recurse.

frames.caption: mid = 0 + (6 - 0)/2 = 3. Probe nums[3] = 7.
frames.note: Compute the midpoint as lo + (hi - lo) / 2, never (lo + hi) / 2 — the naive sum can overflow a 32-bit int for large indices. The closed interval [lo, hi] holds every index still in play, and the loop test is lo <= hi (not <) because a one-element window can still be the hit, and we test equality for that hit.

frames.caption: nums[lo] = 4 <= nums[mid] = 7: the LEFT half [0, 3] = [4,5,6,7] is sorted.
frames.note: The membership test for the sorted half: nums[lo] <= nums[mid] means the left half [lo, mid] is a clean ascending run (no pivot inside it). Once you know a half is sorted you can answer "is target in this half?" with a single range check on its two endpoints — that is the whole trick. Decide membership BEFORE recursing; never recurse into a half you have not proven could contain the target.

frames.caption: Is 0 in [7, 7)? No — discard the left half: lo = mid + 1 = 4.
frames.note: target is not inside the sorted left half, so it cannot be there at all — discard it with lo = mid + 1 (past mid, so the window strictly shrinks and the loop is guaranteed to terminate). Whatever is left to search is the other half, which may contain the pivot but is still half the size — each probe halves the candidates, so we stay O(log n).

frames.caption: mid = 4 + (6 - 4)/2 = 5. Probe nums[5] = 1.
frames.note: Compute the midpoint as lo + (hi - lo) / 2, never (lo + hi) / 2 — the naive sum can overflow a 32-bit int for large indices. The closed interval [lo, hi] holds every index still in play, and the loop test is lo <= hi (not <) because a one-element window can still be the hit, and we test equality for that hit.

frames.caption: nums[lo] = 0 <= nums[mid] = 1: the LEFT half [4, 5] = [0,1] is sorted.
frames.note: The membership test for the sorted half: nums[lo] <= nums[mid] means the left half [lo, mid] is a clean ascending run (no pivot inside it). Once you know a half is sorted you can answer "is target in this half?" with a single range check on its two endpoints — that is the whole trick. Decide membership BEFORE recursing; never recurse into a half you have not proven could contain the target.

frames.caption: Is 0 in [0, 1)? Yes — search the sorted left half: hi = mid - 1 = 4.
frames.note: target falls inside [nums[lo], nums[mid]) on the sorted left half, so the answer — if it exists — must be there. Throw away the right half with hi = mid - 1 and keep narrowing the proven half.

frames.caption: mid = 4 + (4 - 4)/2 = 4. Probe nums[4] = 0.
frames.note: Compute the midpoint as lo + (hi - lo) / 2, never (lo + hi) / 2 — the naive sum can overflow a 32-bit int for large indices. The closed interval [lo, hi] holds every index still in play, and the loop test is lo <= hi (not <) because a one-element window can still be the hit, and we test equality for that hit.

frames.caption: nums[4] = 0 == target. Found it — return index 4.
frames.note: nums[mid] equals target — the win condition, so return the index immediately. Notice this all cost O(log n) time and O(1) space: the rotation never broke the halving, because at every step one half stayed sorted and a single range check told us which way to go.
